---
title: "Worksheet - 4"
author: "Hussain Abdul Kadhir"
date: last-modified
format:
  pdf:
    fontsize: 8pt
    fig.width: 4
    fig.height: 3
    fig.align: "center"
---


```{r setup}
library(car)
library(MASS)

# BOSTON dataset
data(Boston)
head(Boston)
colnames(Boston)

y <- Boston$medv
x1 <- Boston$rm
x3 <- Boston$lstat
x4 <- Boston$age

# a
fit1 <- lm(y ~ x1 +I(x1^2) + x3 + x4)
summary(fit1)
vif(fit1)
# x4 is not significant, so we can remove it and fit the model again
fit2 <- lm(y ~ x1 +I(x1^2) + x3)
summary(fit2)
vif(fit2)

# removing x1
fit3 <- lm(y ~ I(x1^2) + x3)
summary(fit3)
vif(fit3)
# everything is significant
# everything is below 5, so no multicollinearity issue (VIF < 5)


# centering rm_cent <- rm - mean(rm)
x1_cent <- x1 - mean(x1)
fit4 <- lm(y ~ x1_cent + I(x1_cent^2) + x3)
summary(fit4)
vif(fit4)

# this is much better, all variables are significant and no multicollinearity issue (VIF < 5)



```



## Summary of results:
a. Model was fit with requested varaibles. Since, x4 was not significant, it was removed and model was fit again. After removing x4, all variables are significant. 
b. But VIF was high for x1 and x1^2, so x1 was removed and model was fit again. After removing x1, all variables are significant and VIF is below 5, so no multicollinearity issue.
c. The model was fit after centering
d. After centering x1, all variables are significant and VIF is below 5, so no multicollinearity issue. In addition $R^2$ and $R^2_{adj}$ improved
-   `fit3 -> Multiple R-squared:  0.6627,	Adjusted R-squared:  0.6613 `
-   `fit4 -> Multiple R-squared:  0.7318,	Adjusted R-squared:  0.7302 `
